subject: mathematics
ncdc_competency: The learner applies the binomial, rectangular (uniform), and normal probability distributions to model real-life phenomena and compute probabilities for decision making in science, health, and social contexts.

lessons.title: The Binomial Distribution вҖ” Model, Formula, and Properties

content.body: The binomial distribution is the most widely used discrete probability distribution. It models the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials, where each trial has exactly two outcomes вҖ” success or failure вҖ” and the probability of success is the same on every trial. This situation arises constantly in real life: the number of students who pass an exam out of a class of 30, the number of defective items in a batch of 100, the number of patients who respond to a treatment out of 20, and the number of times a coin lands heads in 10 tosses are all binomial scenarios. The distribution was introduced in connection with combinations in Topic 12 and the binomial theorem in Topic 13 вҖ” now we study it as a probability model in its own right.
Conditions for the Binomial Distribution (FIST)

items: Fixed number of trials: the experiment is repeated exactly n times.
Independent trials: the outcome of any one trial does not affect any other trial.
Success/Failure: each trial has exactly two possible outcomes вҖ” success (S) or failure (F).
Two outcomes, constant probability: the probability of success p is the same on every trial. Therefore P(failure) = 1вҲ’p = q (constant).
Memory aid: FIST вҖ” Fixed, Independent, Success/Failure, Two-outcome (constant probability).
If ALL four conditions hold, X = number of successes ~ B(n, p) вҖ” binomial with parameters n and p.

content.body:
Binomial probability formula:
If X ~ B(n, p), then:

P(X = r) = вҒҝCбөЈ pКі (1вҲ’p)вҒҝвҒ»Кі   for r = 0, 1, 2, ..., n

where:
вҖў n = number of trials
вҖў p = probability of success on each trial
вҖў q = 1вҲ’p = probability of failure
вҖў вҒҝCбөЈ = number of ways to choose which r trials are successes

Mean and variance:
E(X) = np
Var(X) = np(1вҲ’p) = npq
Sd(X) = вҲҡ(npq)

Note: The full distribution sums to 1:
ОЈ[r=0 to n] вҒҝCбөЈ pКі qвҒҝвҒ»Кі = (p+q)вҒҝ = 1вҒҝ = 1 вң“  (by the Binomial Theorem)

content.title: Example 1: Computing binomial probabilities
content.body:
A multiple choice test has 10 questions, each with 4 options (one correct). A student guesses every answer. Let X = number of correct answers. X ~ B(10, 1/4).

a) P(X = 3):
= В№вҒ°CвӮғ (1/4)Ві (3/4)вҒ·
= 120 Г— (1/64) Г— (3вҒ·/4вҒ·)
= 120 Г— (2187/16384)
= 120 Г— 2187/16384
= 262,440/16,384
вүҲ 0.2503

b) P(X = 0):
= (3/4)В№вҒ° вүҲ 0.0563

c) P(X вүҘ 1) = 1 вҲ’ P(X=0) = 1 вҲ’ 0.0563 вүҲ 0.9437

d) Mean = np = 10 Г— 1/4 = 2.5
   Variance = npq = 10 Г— (1/4) Г— (3/4) = 30/16 = 15/8 = 1.875
   Sd вүҲ 1.369

content.title: Example 2: Identifying binomial conditions in context
content.body:
Decide whether X ~ B(n, p) in each case:

a) A doctor treats 15 patients with a drug. Each patient independently has P(recovery) = 0.8. X = number who recover.
   FIST check: n=15 вң“, independent вң“, two outcomes (recover/not) вң“, p=0.8 constant вң“.
   X ~ B(15, 0.8) вң“

b) Cards are drawn WITHOUT replacement from a deck. X = number of Aces in 5 draws.
   NOT binomial вҖ” drawing without replacement means trials are NOT independent (the probability changes with each draw).

c) A factory produces bolts. Each bolt independently has P(defective) = 0.05. Bolts are inspected until the first defective one is found. X = number inspected.
   NOT binomial вҖ” n is not fixed (we stop when we find a defective).
   This is a geometric distribution.

d) A fair coin is tossed until 10 Heads appear. X = number of tosses needed.
   NOT binomial вҖ” n is not fixed.

Public health application: During Uganda's vaccination campaigns (e.g. against measles or polio), health workers track vaccination coverage. If 85% of children in a district are vaccinated, and 20 children are randomly selected, X = number vaccinated ~ B(20, 0.85). The Ministry of Health uses P(XвүҘ17) to assess whether target coverage is being met. P(XвүҘ17) = P(17)+P(18)+P(19)+P(20) gives the probability that at least 85% of sampled children are vaccinated.

questions.question: X ~ B(5, 0.4). Find E(X) and Var(X).
Which scenario follows a binomial distribution?
options: Drawing 5 cards from a deck without replacement, X = number of Aces
Tossing a biased coin 12 times, X = number of Heads
Selecting items until a defective one is found
Number of phone calls received in one hour
questions.answer: Tossing a biased coin 12 times, X = number of Heads
questions.explanation: This satisfies all FIST conditions: n=12 fixed, tosses are independent, two outcomes (H/T), constant p. The other options fail: A uses sampling without replacement (not independent); C has no fixed n; D is modelled by Poisson.

questions.question: X ~ B(n, 0.5) with E(X)=6. Find n.
X ~ B(4, 0.6). Find P(X вүҘ 3).
A surgeon has success rate 0.9 per operation. In 5 operations, find P(all 5 succeed).
X ~ B(10, p) has Var(X)=2.1. Find p (taking p<0.5).
questions.explanation: npq=10p(1вҲ’p)=2.1 вҶ’ p(1вҲ’p)=0.21 вҶ’ pВІвҲ’p+0.21=0 вҶ’ (pвҲ’0.3)(pвҲ’0.7)=0 вҶ’ p=0.3 (taking p<0.5).

questions.question: In a B(6, 1/3) distribution, find P(X=0) + P(X=6).

lessons.title: The Binomial Distribution вҖ” Cumulative Probabilities and the Mode

content.body: Once we can compute individual binomial probabilities P(X=r), the next step is computing cumulative probabilities вҖ” the probability that X takes values in a range. These are used in hypothesis testing, quality control, and decision making. We also study the most probable value (mode) of a binomial distribution, the recurrence relation between successive probabilities, and how the binomial approximates other distributions as n grows large.
Cumulative Binomial Probabilities

content.heading: Computing cumulative probabilities:
items: P(X вүӨ r) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) + ... + P(X=r)  вҖ” sum all terms up to r
P(X вүҘ r) = 1 вҲ’ P(X вүӨ rвҲ’1)  вҖ” complement of the cumulative
P(a вүӨ X вүӨ b) = P(X вүӨ b) вҲ’ P(X вүӨ aвҲ’1)
Always consider whether the complement will be faster: P(X вүҘ 1) = 1вҲ’P(X=0) is much quicker than adding all other terms.
For large n, cumulative binomial tables are used. In examinations, you may be asked to compute or read from tables.

content.body: The Recurrence Relation

The ratio of successive probabilities:

P(X = r+1) / P(X = r) = [(nвҲ’r)/(r+1)] Г— [p/q]

Derivation:
P(X=r+1) = вҒҝCбөЈвӮҠвӮҒ pКівҒәВ№ qвҒҝвҒ»КівҒ»В№
P(X=r)   = вҒҝCбөЈ pКі qвҒҝвҒ»Кі

Ratio = [вҒҝCбөЈвӮҠвӮҒ/вҒҝCбөЈ] Г— (p/q) = [(nвҲ’r)/(r+1)] Г— (p/q)

This ratio > 1 (i.e. P increases) when:
(nвҲ’r)p > (r+1)q
np вҲ’ rp > rq + q
np вҲ’ q > r(p+q) = r
r < np вҲ’ q

Probabilities increase until r < npвҲ’q, then decrease.
The MODE (most probable value) is the integer(s) where P(X=r) is maximised.

content.title: Example 2: Finding the mode of a binomial distribution
content.body:
X ~ B(12, 0.4). Find the mode.

Compute npвҲ’q = 12(0.4)вҲ’0.6 = 4.8вҲ’0.6 = 4.2

Probabilities increase while r < 4.2, i.e. for r = 0,1,2,3,4.
Probabilities decrease from r = 5 onwards.

So the mode is r = 4 (the largest integer below the turning point when the distribution switches from increasing to decreasing).

Verification:
P(X=4) = В№ВІCвӮ„(0.4)вҒҙ(0.6)вҒё = 495Г—0.0256Г—0.01679... вүҲ 0.2128
P(X=5) = В№ВІCвӮ…(0.4)вҒө(0.6)вҒ· = 792Г—0.01024Г—0.02799... вүҲ 0.2270

Hmm вҖ” P(5) > P(4), so mode = 5? Let me recheck:
np = 4.8. Mode = floor(np+p) = floor(4.8+0.4) = floor(5.2) = 5.

Alternative rule: the mode is floor((n+1)p) when (n+1)p is not an integer.
floor(13Г—0.4) = floor(5.2) = 5. Mode = 5. вң“

content.title: Example 3: Using the binomial distribution for decision making
content.body:
A manufacturer claims 90% of products pass quality control. A buyer tests 10 randomly selected items. The buyer will reject the batch if fewer than 8 pass.

X ~ B(10, 0.9). Find P(batch is rejected) = P(X < 8) = P(X вүӨ 7).

P(X вүӨ 7) = 1 вҲ’ P(X вүҘ 8) = 1 вҲ’ [P(X=8)+P(X=9)+P(X=10)]

P(X=8) = В№вҒ°CвӮҲ(0.9)вҒё(0.1)ВІ = 45Г—0.43047Г—0.01 = 0.19371
P(X=9) = В№вҒ°CвӮү(0.9)вҒ№(0.1) = 10Г—0.38742Г—0.1 = 0.38742
P(X=10) = (0.9)В№вҒ° = 0.34868

P(X вүҘ 8) = 0.19371+0.38742+0.34868 = 0.92981

P(batch rejected) = P(X вүӨ 7) = 1 вҲ’ 0.92981 = 0.07019 вүҲ 7%

Conclusion: Even with a 90% pass rate, there is still about a 7% chance the buyer rejects the batch вҖ” a real business risk.

Quality control in Ugandan manufacturing: The Uganda National Bureau of Standards (UNBS) certifies products for quality. When UNBS inspectors test a batch of say 20 items and the manufacturer claims a defect rate of only 2%, UNBS uses the binomial distribution to determine the probability of finding 2 or more defects (which would trigger a recall). If this probability is high enough to be concerning, it provides statistical evidence that the manufacturer's claimed defect rate is too optimistic.

questions.question: X ~ B(5, 0.3). Find P(X вүҘ 2).
X ~ B(8, 0.5). Find P(X = 4) вҖ” the most likely value.
questions.explanation: P(X=4)=вҒёCвӮ„(0.5)вҒё=70Г—(1/256)=70/256=35/128вүҲ0.2734. By symmetry, this is the mode when p=0.5.

questions.question: Using P(X=r+1)/P(X=r) = [(nвҲ’r)/(r+1)](p/q), for X~B(10,0.4), the ratio at r=3 is:
questions.explanation: Ratio=[(10вҲ’3)/(3+1)]Г—(0.4/0.6)=(7/4)Г—(2/3)=14/12=7/6>1. So P(X=4)>P(X=3) вҖ” probabilities still increasing at r=3.

questions.question: Find the mode of X ~ B(15, 0.6).
X~B(20,0.1). P(X=0)=(0.9)ВІвҒ°вүҲ0.1216. Find P(XвүҘ1) to 4 d.p.
X~B(n,0.5) has P(X=0)=1/32. Find n and P(X=1).
X~B(4, p) with P(X=2)=6pВІ(1вҲ’p)ВІ. If p=2/3, find P(X=2).

lessons.title: The Normal Distribution вҖ” Shape, Parameters, and the Standard Normal

content.body: The normal distribution is the single most important probability distribution in all of statistics. It is the bell-shaped curve that describes the distribution of countless naturally occurring measurements вҖ” heights and weights of people, exam scores, measurement errors, rainfall amounts, and many more. The reason for its ubiquity is the Central Limit Theorem: regardless of the underlying distribution of a population, the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases. The normal distribution is also the limiting form of the binomial distribution for large n. Understanding the normal distribution is essential for all statistical inference, hypothesis testing, and quality control.
Properties of the Normal Distribution

items: The normal distribution N(Ој, ПғВІ) is characterised by two parameters: mean Ој (location) and variance ПғВІ (spread).
The PDF is: f(x) = [1/(ПғвҲҡ(2ПҖ))] Г— exp[вҲ’(xвҲ’Ој)ВІ/(2ПғВІ)] for вҲ’вҲһ < x < вҲһ.
Bell-shaped, symmetric about the mean Ој.
Mean = Median = Mode = Ој (all three measures of centre coincide).
Total area under the curve = 1.
The curve is asymptotic вҖ” it never touches the x-axis.
The inflection points (where the curve changes from concave to convex) occur at x = ОјВұПғ.
Empirical rule (68-95-99.7 rule): P(ОјвҲ’Пғ < X < Ој+Пғ) вүҲ 0.6827, P(ОјвҲ’2Пғ < X < Ој+2Пғ) вүҲ 0.9545, P(ОјвҲ’3Пғ < X < Ој+3Пғ) вүҲ 0.9973.
Larger Пғ вҶ’ flatter and wider curve. Smaller Пғ вҶ’ taller and narrower curve. Mean Ој shifts the curve left or right.

content.body: The Standard Normal Distribution Z ~ N(0, 1)

The standard normal distribution has Ој=0 and ПғВІ=1.

Standardisation (Z-score transformation):
If X ~ N(Ој, ПғВІ), then Z = (XвҲ’Ој)/Пғ ~ N(0, 1)

This converts any normal distribution to the standard normal,
allowing probabilities to be read from standard normal tables (z-tables).

Notation:
ОҰ(z) = P(Z вүӨ z) = the standard normal CDF

Key symmetry properties of ОҰ:
ОҰ(0) = 0.5  (the mean divides the distribution in half)
ОҰ(вҲ’z) = 1 вҲ’ ОҰ(z)  (left tail = 1 вҲ’ right tail by symmetry)
P(Z > z) = 1 вҲ’ ОҰ(z)
P(Z < вҲ’z) = ОҰ(вҲ’z) = 1 вҲ’ ОҰ(z)
P(a < Z < b) = ОҰ(b) вҲ’ ОҰ(a)
P(|Z| < z) = 2ОҰ(z) вҲ’ 1

content.title: Example 1: Using z-tables to find probabilities
Example 2: Standardising X ~ N(Ој, ПғВІ) to find probabilities
content.body:
The heights of S5 students at a Kampala school are normally distributed with mean 168 cm and standard deviation 8 cm. X ~ N(168, 64).

a) P(X < 180):
Z = (180вҲ’168)/8 = 12/8 = 1.5
P(X < 180) = P(Z < 1.5) = ОҰ(1.5) = 0.9332

b) P(160 < X < 176):
For X=160: Z = (160вҲ’168)/8 = вҲ’1.0
For X=176: Z = (176вҲ’168)/8 = 1.0
P(160 < X < 176) = ОҰ(1.0) вҲ’ ОҰ(вҲ’1.0) = 0.8413 вҲ’ 0.1587 = 0.6826
(This is the '68% within 1Пғ' rule!)

c) P(X > 190):
Z = (190вҲ’168)/8 = 22/8 = 2.75
P(X > 190) = 1 вҲ’ ОҰ(2.75) = 1 вҲ’ 0.9970 = 0.0030
Only 0.3% of students are taller than 190 cm.

Why measurements are approximately normal: When a measurement (like height) is the sum of many small independent factors (genetics, nutrition, sleep, exercise, etc.), the Central Limit Theorem guarantees its distribution will be approximately normal. This is why UNEB exam scores across Uganda, blood pressure readings at Mulago Hospital, and crop yield measurements by NAADS all tend to follow approximately normal distributions. The normal distribution is not just a mathematical convenience вҖ” it is a natural consequence of how many phenomena arise from accumulated independent effects.

questions.question: X ~ N(50, 25). Find P(X < 55) using Z = (XвҲ’Ој)/Пғ. (ОҰ(1)=0.8413)
For N(Ој,ПғВІ), the inflection points of the PDF are at:
questions.explanation: The PDF f(x) has inflection points (where concavity changes) at x=ОјВұПғ. These are visually where the bell shape transitions from 'concave down' to 'concave up'.

questions.question: X ~ N(Ој, ПғВІ). Which of the following is always true?
options: The mean equals the median equals the mode
questions.explanation: The normal distribution is symmetric about Ој, so P(X>Ој)=P(X<Ој)=0.5. Also, for a symmetric unimodal distribution, mean=median=mode=Ој. Both A and C are always true for any N(Ој,ПғВІ).

questions.question: X ~ N(200, 100). About what percentage of values lie between 180 and 220?
questions.explanation: Пғ=10. 180=200вҲ’2Пғ and 220=200+2Пғ. By the empirical rule, P(ОјвҲ’2Пғ<X<Ој+2Пғ)вүҲ95.45%вүҲ95%.

questions.question: P(Z < zвӮҖ) = 0.9772. Find zвӮҖ.

lessons.title: Normal Distribution вҖ” Inverse Problems and Finding Parameters

content.body: In the previous lesson, we found probabilities given values. Now we reverse the process: given a probability, find the value (or parameter). This inverse normal problem is fundamental to setting standards вҖ” 'what score separates the top 5% of students?', 'what weight must an item exceed to be in the heaviest 1%?', 'what blood pressure reading corresponds to the 90th percentile?'. We also solve problems where the parameters Ој and Пғ are unknown, using the standardisation equation to set up simultaneous equations.
Inverse Normal вҖ” Finding a Value Given a Probability

To find x given P(X < x) = p where X ~ N(Ој, ПғВІ):

Step 1: Find z such that ОҰ(z) = p  (read z from standard normal tables in reverse)
Step 2: Back-transform: x = Ој + zПғ

Useful critical values (memorise these):
ОҰ(1.645) = 0.95    вҶ’  P(X < Ој+1.645Пғ) = 0.95
ОҰ(1.960) = 0.975   вҶ’  P(X < Ој+1.96Пғ)  = 0.975
ОҰ(2.326) = 0.99    вҶ’  P(X < Ој+2.326Пғ) = 0.99
ОҰ(2.576) = 0.995   вҶ’  P(X < Ој+2.576Пғ) = 0.995

For lower-tail values (probability < 0.5):
P(X < ОјвҲ’1.645Пғ) = 0.05  (use negative z-score)
P(X < ОјвҲ’1.96Пғ)  = 0.025

content.title: Example 1: Finding a value from a given probability
content.body:
UACE examination scores are normally distributed with mean 14 points and standard deviation 3 points. X ~ N(14, 9).

a) Find the score that separates the top 10% of candidates.
P(X > x) = 0.10  вҶ’  P(X < x) = 0.90
ОҰ(z) = 0.90 вҶ’ z = 1.282
x = Ој + zПғ = 14 + 1.282Г—3 = 14 + 3.846 вүҲ 17.85
Students who score above 17.85 points are in the top 10%.

b) Find the score below which 25% of students fall (1st quartile QвӮҒ).
ОҰ(z) = 0.25 вҶ’ z = вҲ’0.674 (negative since below mean)
x = 14 + (вҲ’0.674)Г—3 = 14 вҲ’ 2.022 вүҲ 11.98
QвӮҒ вүҲ 11.98 points.
Finding Unknown Parameters Ој and Пғ

When two probabilities are given for the same distribution with unknown Ој and Пғ, we set up two equations by standardising each given condition. This gives a simultaneous equation system for Ој and Пғ.

content.title: Example 2: Finding Ој and Пғ from two probability conditions
content.body:
X ~ N(Ој, ПғВІ). Given P(X < 25) = 0.2266 and P(X < 35) = 0.8413.

Step 1 вҖ” Convert to z-scores:
P(X < 25) = 0.2266 вҶ’ ОҰ(zвӮҒ) = 0.2266 вҶ’ zвӮҒ = вҲ’0.75
(since ОҰ(0.75)=0.7734, ОҰ(вҲ’0.75)=1вҲ’0.7734=0.2266)

P(X < 35) = 0.8413 вҶ’ ОҰ(zвӮӮ) = 0.8413 вҶ’ zвӮӮ = 1.0

Step 2 вҖ” Write two equations:
(25вҲ’Ој)/Пғ = вҲ’0.75   вҶ’ 25 = Ој вҲ’ 0.75Пғ  ...(1)
(35вҲ’Ој)/Пғ = 1.00    вҶ’ 35 = Ој + Пғ       ...(2)

Step 3 вҖ” Subtract (1) from (2):
10 = 1.75Пғ вҶ’ Пғ = 10/1.75 = 40/7 вүҲ 5.714

From (2): Ој = 35 вҲ’ Пғ = 35 вҲ’ 5.714 вүҲ 29.286

Verification:
P(X<25): z=(25вҲ’29.286)/5.714=вҲ’0.75 вҶ’ ОҰ(вҲ’0.75)=0.2266 вң“
P(X<35): z=(35вҲ’29.286)/5.714=1.00 вҶ’ ОҰ(1)=0.8413 вң“

content.title: Example 3: Symmetric probability interval
content.body:
X ~ N(50, ПғВІ). Given P(45 < X < 55) = 0.7286. Find Пғ.

By symmetry about Ој=50, both 45 and 55 are equidistant from the mean:
Z for X=55: z = (55вҲ’50)/Пғ = 5/Пғ
Z for X=45: z = (45вҲ’50)/Пғ = вҲ’5/Пғ

P(вҲ’5/Пғ < Z < 5/Пғ) = 0.7286
2ОҰ(5/Пғ) вҲ’ 1 = 0.7286
ОҰ(5/Пғ) = 0.8643
5/Пғ = 1.10  (from tables: ОҰ(1.10) вүҲ 0.8643)
Пғ = 5/1.10 вүҲ 4.545

Medical screening (Uganda context): Mulago National Referral Hospital screens patients for hypertension. Blood pressure X ~ N(120, 225) mmHg (Ој=120, Пғ=15). The threshold for 'high blood pressure' is set at the 90th percentile. Using the inverse normal: z=1.282 вҶ’ threshold = 120+1.282Г—15 вүҲ 139.2 mmHg. A doctor can also compute: if a patient's reading is 150 mmHg, their z-score = (150вҲ’120)/15 = 2.0, so only 2.28% of the population has higher pressure вҖ” alerting the doctor to a serious condition.

questions.question: Exam scores X~N(55,64). Find the minimum score to be in the top 5%. (ОҰ(1.645)=0.95)
X~N(100,100). Find c such that P(100вҲ’c<X<100+c)=0.9544. (Empirical rule)
Find z such that P(Z > z) = 0.025. (ОҰ(1.96)=0.975)

lessons.title: Normal Approximation to the Binomial and Continuity Correction

content.body: For large n, computing exact binomial probabilities becomes very tedious вҖ” imagine computing P(X=47) for X~B(100,0.4). The normal distribution provides a powerful approximation in this case. When n is large and p is not too extreme, the binomial distribution B(n,p) can be approximated by N(np, npq). However, since the binomial is discrete and the normal is continuous, we need to apply the continuity correction вҖ” adjusting the boundaries by Вұ0.5 to account for the gap between the two types of distributions. This approximation is a key examination topic and a fundamental technique in statistics.
When to Use the Normal Approximation

items: Rule of thumb: the normal approximation to B(n,p) is valid when BOTH npвүҘ5 AND nq=n(1вҲ’p)вүҘ5.
The approximation works best when p is close to 0.5 (symmetric) and n is large.
If p is close to 0 or 1, the Poisson approximation may be more appropriate (covered in more advanced courses).
The approximation: X~B(n,p) вүҲ Y~N(Ој,ПғВІ) where Ој=np and ПғВІ=npq.
Always state the parameters: 'Approximate X~B(n,p) by Y~N(np,npq)'.

content.body: The Continuity Correction

Continuity correction вҖ” adjusting discrete boundaries to continuous:

Discrete (Binomial)  вҶ’  Continuous (Normal)
P(X = r)             вҶ’  P(rвҲ’0.5 < Y < r+0.5)
P(X вүӨ r)             вҶ’  P(Y < r+0.5)
P(X < r)             вҶ’  P(Y < rвҲ’0.5)
P(X вүҘ r)             вҶ’  P(Y > rвҲ’0.5)
P(X > r)             вҶ’  P(Y > r+0.5)
P(a вүӨ X вүӨ b)         вҶ’  P(aвҲ’0.5 < Y < b+0.5)

Memory tip:
'вүӨ' and 'вүҘ' go OUTWARD (away from centre) by 0.5
'<' and '>' go INWARD (toward centre) by 0.5
The correction WIDENS вүӨ/вүҘ inequalities and NARROWS </>  inequalities

content.title: Example 1: Normal approximation with continuity correction
content.body:
X ~ B(100, 0.4). Find P(X вүӨ 45) using a normal approximation.

Step 1 вҖ” Check conditions:
np = 40 вүҘ 5 вң“. nq = 60 вүҘ 5 вң“. Approximation valid.

Step 2 вҖ” Find parameters:
Ој = np = 100Г—0.4 = 40
ПғВІ = npq = 100Г—0.4Г—0.6 = 24
Пғ = вҲҡ24 = 2вҲҡ6 вүҲ 4.899

Step 3 вҖ” Apply continuity correction:
P(X вүӨ 45) вҶ’ P(Y < 45.5) [вүӨ goes outward, +0.5]

Step 4 вҖ” Standardise:
Z = (45.5 вҲ’ 40)/4.899 = 5.5/4.899 вүҲ 1.123
P(Y < 45.5) = ОҰ(1.123) вүҲ ОҰ(1.12) вүҲ 0.8686

Comparison: exact binomial value вүҲ 0.8644 вҖ” the approximation is quite close.

content.title: Example 3: Real-world application of normal approximation
content.body:
A survey finds that 65% of Ugandan adults own a mobile phone. In a random sample of 80 adults:

X ~ B(80, 0.65). Check: np=52вүҘ5 вң“, nq=28вүҘ5 вң“.
Approximate: Y ~ N(52, 18.2). (Ој=52, ПғВІ=80Г—0.65Г—0.35=18.2, ПғвүҲ4.266)

a) P(X вүҘ 55): P(Y > 54.5)
Z = (54.5вҲ’52)/4.266 = 2.5/4.266 вүҲ 0.586
P вүҲ 1вҲ’ОҰ(0.586) вүҲ 1вҲ’0.721 = 0.279

b) P(50 вүӨ X вүӨ 60): P(49.5 < Y < 60.5)
ZвӮҒ = (49.5вҲ’52)/4.266 вүҲ вҲ’0.586
ZвӮӮ = (60.5вҲ’52)/4.266 вүҲ 1.993
P вүҲ ОҰ(1.993)вҲ’ОҰ(вҲ’0.586) вүҲ 0.977вҲ’0.279 = 0.698

Historical context: Before computers, statisticians relied on pre-computed normal tables for ALL their probability calculations вҖ” including binomial, Poisson, and other discrete distributions. The normal approximation to the binomial was therefore one of the most practically important tools in all of applied statistics, allowing manageable calculations on large datasets. Today, computers compute exact binomial probabilities instantly, but understanding the approximation reveals the deep connection between discrete and continuous distributions and why the normal distribution occupies such a central place in statistics.

questions.question: X~B(50,0.4). The normal approximation parameters are:
X~B(100,0.5). Using continuity correction, P(XвүӨ55) is approximated by P(Y<__).
questions.explanation: P(XвүӨ55) with continuity correction: вүӨ goes outward (+0.5) вҶ’ P(Y<55.5).

questions.question: The rule of thumb for normal approximation to B(n,p) requires:
questions.explanation: Both npвүҘ5 and nq=n(1вҲ’p)вүҘ5 must hold for the normal approximation to be reasonably accurate.

questions.question: X~B(n,p) approximated by Y~N(40,24). Find n and p.
X~B(80,0.3). Approximate P(X=24) using continuity correction (Ој=24, ПғВІ=16.8, ПғвүҲ4.099).
questions.explanation: P(X=r) with continuity correction вҶ’ P(rвҲ’0.5<Y<r+0.5). For r=24: P(23.5<Y<24.5).

questions.question: X~B(200,0.4) ~ Y~N(80,48). Find P(X<75) approximately. (ОҰ(0.72)вүҲ0.764)
When applying continuity correction to P(XвүҘr), the normal probability becomes:
questions.explanation: P(XвүҘr) includes r itself. The continuity correction moves OUTWARD (away from centre), which for вүҘ means subtracting 0.5. So P(XвүҘr)вҶ’P(Y>rвҲ’0.5).

questions.question: Why is a continuity correction needed when approximating B(n,p) by N(np,npq)?
options: The binomial has a different mean from the normal
The binomial is discrete (probability at exact integers) while the normal is continuous (probability at exact points is 0)
The normal distribution is always an overestimate
The parameters n and p must be adjusted
questions.answer: The binomial is discrete (probability at exact integers) while the normal is continuous (probability at exact points is 0)
questions.explanation: The binomial assigns probability to exact integer values (e.g. P(X=5) can be non-zero). The continuous normal assigns zero probability to any exact value. The continuity correction bridges this by replacing each integer r with the interval (rвҲ’0.5, r+0.5), which has non-zero normal probability.

key_concepts: Binomial X~B(n,p): conditions FIST (Fixed, Independent, Success/Failure, Two-outcome). P(X=r)=вҒҝCбөЈpКіqвҒҝвҒ»Кі. E(X)=np. Var(X)=npq.
Cumulative binomial: P(XвүӨr)=sum of individual terms. Use complement P(XвүҘr)=1вҲ’P(XвүӨrвҲ’1) for efficiency.
Normal distribution N(Ој,ПғВІ): bell-shaped, symmetric about Ој. Mean=Median=Mode=Ој. Inflections at ОјВұПғ. Empirical rule: 68-95-99.7% within 1,2,3 standard deviations.
Standardisation: Z=(XвҲ’Ој)/Пғ ~ N(0,1). P(X<x)=ОҰ((xвҲ’Ој)/Пғ). Symmetry: ОҰ(вҲ’z)=1вҲ’ОҰ(z).
Inverse normal: given P(X<x)=p, find z from tables then x=Ој+zПғ.
Finding parameters: use two given probabilities вҶ’ two equations via standardisation вҶ’ solve simultaneously.
Normal approximation to binomial: valid when npвүҘ5 AND nqвүҘ5. Approximate B(n,p) by N(np,npq).
common_errors: Forgetting to check FIST conditions before applying the binomial model.
Using np as the variance instead of npq вҖ” variance of B(n,p) is npq, not np.
Forgetting the continuity correction when approximating binomial by normal.
Applying continuity correction in the wrong direction вҖ” вүӨ adds 0.5 (outward), < subtracts 0.5 (inward).
Standardising with wrong sign вҖ” for lower-tail probabilities, z is negative: ОҰ(вҲ’z)=1вҲ’ОҰ(z).
Forgetting to square Пғ: N(Ој,ПғВІ) uses ПғВІ as the second parameter, not Пғ.
Using the approximation when np<5 or nq<5 вҖ” the normal approximation is unreliable then.

topic_quiz.title: Topic 15 End-of-Topic Assessment вҖ” Probability Distributions

questions.question: X~B(10,0.3). Find P(X=3) to 4 d.p.
X~B(12,0.5). Find E(X), Var(X), and the mode.
X~B(100,0.5). Normal approximation: Y~N(50,25). P(XвүҘ55) вүҲ P(Y>54.5). Find this probability. (ОҰ(0.9)=0.8159)
Which is NOT a condition for X~B(n,p)?
questions.explanation: The binomial conditions (FIST) are: Fixed n, Independent trials, Success/Failure outcomes, and Two-outcome (constant probability p). The binomial is a DISCRETE distribution вҖ” outcomes do not need to be normally distributed.

questions.question: X~B(50,0.6). E(X)=30, Var(X)=12. Normal approximation Y~N(30,12). Find P(X=30) using continuity correction. (ОҰ(0.14)вүҲ0.5557)
X~N(70,100). Find the 95th percentile. (ОҰ(1.645)=0.95)
X~B(n,p) with np=30 and npq=21. Find n and p.
A binomial X~B(200,0.45) can be approximated by N(90,49.5). P(X>100) is approximated by P(Y>100.5). Find Z.
X~N(Ој,ПғВІ) is symmetric, so P(X<Ој)=
questions.explanation: The normal distribution is symmetric about its mean Ој. Therefore exactly half the distribution lies below the mean: P(X<Ој)=0.5, regardless of the values of Ој or Пғ.

questions.question: A coin is tossed 120 times. Use the normal approximation to find P(between 55 and 65 heads inclusive). Parameters: N(60,30), Пғ=вҲҡ30вүҲ5.477. (ОҰ(0.91)вүҲ0.8186)
Explain why the mode of B(n,p) equals floor((n+1)p) when (n+1)p is not an integer.
options: Because probabilities increase until rвүҘ(n+1)p then decrease вҖ” the transition point determines the mode
Because the binomial is symmetric about its mean
Because the normal approximation has its peak at the mean
Because вҒҝCбөЈ is largest when r=n/2
questions.answer: Because probabilities increase until rвүҘ(n+1)p then decrease вҖ” the transition point determines the mode
questions.explanation: The recurrence ratio P(r+1)/P(r)>1 when r<(n+1)pвҲ’1, equals 1 when r+1=(n+1)p exactly (two equal modes), and <1 when rвүҘ(n+1)p. So probabilities peak at floor((n+1)p) вҖ” the largest integer just before the ratio drops below 1.
